Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I'd study at set times, but with 20 online courses and barely any money for proper resources, I ended up cramming whenever I could find a moment. I spent around 23 hours a week studying, but it felt chaotic—like I was always catching up rather than staying ahead. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety was through the roof, just trying to absorb as much as I could before exhaustion hit.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd be consistent for a few days, then everything would fall apart when multiple assignments piled up across different courses. I couldn't afford to miss work shifts, so my study schedule had to bend around that too. It was more reactive than planned—I studied when panic set in rather than when it made logical sense.
Q3: Usually it was anxiety that drove me to log in—this nagging feeling that I was missing something important or falling behind. Sometimes I'd get notification emails that would trigger me to check in. Other times I'd log in just to watch videos because reading felt too demanding when I was exhausted. The fear of missing a deadline was probably my biggest motivator, even though I rarely actually submitted assignments on time.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic—I'd wake up and check the LMS like it was social media. I think it happened because I was trying to stay on top of things after a particularly bad anxiety episode. But that routine crumbled when life got more complicated with work and personal stuff. It never really stuck as a permanent habit for me.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline—I watched over 200 minutes of them because I could absorb information without having to actively process text when I was mentally drained. Discussion posts were also helpful because seeing other students' perspectives made me feel less alone in my confusion. The reading materials were useful too, but I often had to download them and study offline because my internet at home isn't always reliable.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the assignments—my completion rate was terrible, like barely anything got submitted. It wasn't that I didn't care; I just got so overwhelmed trying to juggle everything that assignments felt impossible to complete properly. I'd start them, get anxious about doing them wrong, and then avoid them entirely. The peer interaction features I also underused because I felt embarrassed about how behind I was.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or understanding from it. Watching a video and having that 'aha' moment made me feel like I was actually learning something. But if something required multiple steps or felt like busywork without clear payoff, my brain would just shut down. With my anxiety levels, I needed quick wins to keep going.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on, even when they seemed interesting. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that made them feel heavier, more stressful. Ironically, I sometimes engaged more with optional content because there was no pressure attached—I could explore without fear of failing. The required stuff just triggered my anxiety more intensely.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, lecture slides, and any materials I could access and then study them on my phone during bus rides or work breaks. I couldn't always afford reliable internet, so offline studying was essential for me. I also used free textbook PDFs I found online and YouTube videos to supplement what the course offered.
Q10: There were countless nights where I'd be going through printed notes or downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd study with classmates in person sometimes, quizzing each other, and none of that showed up anywhere. My exam scores—96.87—didn't come from nowhere, but if you looked at my LMS activity alone, you'd think I barely tried. The real learning happened in the margins of my life.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or reading once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never touch the LMS version again. My notes became my primary resource because I could annotate them and highlight things that mattered. Going back to the LMS felt redundant when I already had everything I needed saved locally. It was more efficient given my limited internet access.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety spiked to the point where I'd open the LMS, see all the tasks I'd fallen behind on, and just close it immediately. I was working extra shifts because money was tight, and I was barely sleeping. The sadness I've been feeling made it hard to find energy for anything beyond survival mode. That's when my attendance really dropped off.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work or hadn't eaten properly because funds were low, the LMS became this overwhelming monster I couldn't face. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid them entirely rather than ask for help. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS got whatever scraps of attention I had left, which wasn't much. My cognitive load was maxed out most of the time.
Q14: So many times. I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills, replaying anxious thoughts, or just too tired to process anything. I'd watch videos on autopilot without retaining information and have to rewatch them later. Being present in the LMS didn't mean I was actually learning; sometimes I was just going through motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were destroying my completion rate. The anxiety around doing them 'wrong' was paralyzing—I'd rather not submit than submit something imperfect. I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow never came. It's this vicious cycle where avoidance temporarily relieved anxiety but made everything worse long-term.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense panic spikes where I'd suddenly become hyperactive on the LMS. I'd log in frantically, watch videos at 2x speed, try to absorb everything at once. But by then it was usually too late to actually complete assignments properly. Deadlines were more about damage control than productive engagement for me. They ruled my life but not in a healthy way.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it affected me deeply—sometimes motivationally, sometimes devastatingly. Positive feedback on exams made me feel like maybe I wasn't a complete failure. But feedback on incomplete assignments just reinforced my shame spiral. I started avoiding checking feedback because I couldn't handle more evidence of my shortcomings. It's complicated.
Q18: Having 20 courses meant the structure was impossible to manage properly. Each course had its own logic, its own deadlines, its own expectations. I couldn't develop a rhythm with any single course because I was constantly context-switching. The structure demanded more organizational capacity than I had available. A simpler structure with fewer competing demands would have helped enormously.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder—it felt infinite and unconquerable. But the video lectures being available anytime was a lifesaver for my unpredictable schedule. Discussion boards helped when I felt isolated. What made it harder was when materials weren't downloadable or when assignments had unclear rubrics. Flexibility helped; rigidity hurt.
Q20: That's literally me. I think it happens when you're trying desperately to learn and keep up but can't translate that effort into submitted work. The anxiety around assignments can be separate from the desire to understand the material. You might be deeply engaged intellectually but completely blocked when it comes to producing output. High activity can mask a struggle that numbers don't capture.
Q21: That also resonates with me, weirdly. My exam score was excellent despite terrible LMS metrics because I studied extensively offline. Some students are efficient—they don't need constant LMS presence to learn effectively. Or they have external resources, study groups, or learning methods that don't register digitally. Low activity doesn't mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see if someone's crying while staring at a screen or if they're deeply focused. They miss offline study, mental health struggles, financial stress, everything that shapes how a student actually experiences learning. They're a shadow of reality.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm genuinely curious, asking questions in my head, making connections to other things I know. For me, engagement is often invisible—it happens in my thoughts while I'm on the bus or lying awake at night processing concepts. It's not about activity metrics; it's about intellectual and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS can't show that I'm dealing with high anxiety and sadness while trying to succeed academically. It can't show that I'm working to pay rent while taking 20 courses. It doesn't capture the CBT sessions I'm doing to manage my mental health or the hours I spend studying downloaded materials. My distinction grade came from invisible effort, resilience, and struggle that no log file could ever record.
